treatments.name: Definitive Radiation Therapy (66-70 Gy, conventional fractionation)

evidence_level.rationale: Decades of prospective and retrospective data supporting definitive RT as primary treatment for T2N0 glottic cancer. NCCN Category 1 recommendation. Multiple large institutional series with 20+ year follow-up confirm 5-year OS of 79-96%.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year overall survival 79-96% for T2N0 glottic SCC. 5-year local control 65-80% with RT alone. 10-year voice preservation rate of 87.8-90.3%. Stage II glottic cancer has inherently favorable prognosis; RT delivers excellent cure rates with organ preservation.

accessibility.rationale: Universally available at all radiation oncology centers worldwide. Standard of care per NCCN, ESMO, and ASCO guidelines. No special equipment beyond standard linear accelerator required.

safety_profile.rationale: Acute toxicities include mucositis (grade 2-3 in 30-50%), odynophagia, hoarseness, and skin erythema. Late toxicities include xerostomia, laryngeal edema, fibrosis, and chondronecrosis (<5%). Well-tolerated compared to combined modality approaches. Voice outcomes generally good.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker selection required — radiation is effective regardless of PD-L1 or EGFR status. EGFR overexpression and PD-L1 status are not predictive for radiation response in early-stage disease.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Ionizing radiation causes direct and indirect DNA damage through double-strand breaks and free radical generation, leading to tumor cell death. Delivered as intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) or 3D conformal techniques to the larynx, sparing surrounding normal tissues.

key_evidence.study_name: Cleveland Clinic 20-year experience — Definitive RT for T1-T2 Glottic SCC

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — universally available at all radiation oncology centers

treatments.name: Transoral Laser Microsurgery (TLM / CO2 Laser Cordectomy)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple systematic reviews and meta-analyses comparing TLM to RT for T1-T2 glottic cancer. 2025 Laryngoscope meta-analysis (Vasudevan et al.) demonstrated superior 5-year OS with TLM (84.5% vs 79.4%). Accepted NCCN-listed alternative to RT.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 84.5% vs 79.4% for RT. 5-year local control rate 77.2% (TLM) vs 71.6% (RT). 5-year laryngeal preservation rate 91.1%. Incidence of total laryngectomy only 1.8% in TLM group vs 11.8% in RT group. T2a 5-year local control 75.2%.

accessibility.rationale: Available at most academic medical centers and many community hospitals with experienced head and neck surgeons. Requires specialized equipment (CO2 laser, operating microscope) and surgical expertise. Less widely available than RT in rural settings.

safety_profile.rationale: Minimally invasive with low complication rates. No external incision. Short hospital stay (often same-day). 40% normal voice post-operatively, 45% mild-moderate voice change. Avoids radiation toxicity entirely. Risk of need for repeat procedures for positive margins.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker selection required. Surgical excision is effective regardless of PD-L1 or EGFR expression. Treatment selection based on tumor anatomy and extent, not molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: CO2 laser is used transorally under microscopic visualization to precisely excise the tumor from the vocal cord(s). The laser vaporizes tissue with minimal thermal damage to surrounding structures, allowing precise resection while preserving uninvolved laryngeal framework.

key_evidence.study_name: TLM vs RT for T1-T2 Glottic Cancer with AC Involvement — Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

notable_side_effects: Need for repeat procedure if margins positive
treatments.availability: Available at academic medical centers and experienced community hospitals

treatments.name: Concurrent Chemoradiation (Cisplatin + RT 70 Gy)

evidence_level.rationale: RTOG 91-11 landmark trial established concurrent CRT for laryngeal preservation. Multiple studies confirm superior locoregional control in T2 glottic cancer with CRT vs RT alone: 5-year LRC 88.5% vs 72.8%. However, primarily studied in locally advanced disease; evidence for T2N0 is more limited.

survival_benefit.rationale: 10-year survival rate 95.3% with CRT in T2N0M0 glottic cancer. 10-year larynx preservation rate 75.1%. 88% laryngeal preservation at 2 years (RTOG 91-11). Superior locoregional control compared to RT alone. May represent overtreatment for favorable T2N0 patients.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin and radiation are universally available. Standard regimen of cisplatin 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 22, 43 with concurrent RT is well-established. Available at all oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Significantly more toxic than RT alone. Grade 3+ mucositis in 40-60%, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, nausea/vomiting, weight loss, increased late dysphagia and laryngeal dysfunction. May be overtreatment for T2N0 glottic cancer where RT alone achieves excellent outcomes.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker selection required. Cisplatin radiosensitization is independent of PD-L1 and EGFR status. Benefit is greatest in locally advanced disease where locoregional failure risk is higher.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin acts as a radiosensitizer by forming DNA crosslinks that inhibit repair of radiation-induced DNA damage. Concurrent delivery maximizes the synergistic cytotoxic effect. Standard dosing: cisplatin 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 22, and 43 with 70 Gy in 35 fractions.

key_evidence.study_name: RTOG 91-11 — Concurrent CRT for Laryngeal Preservation

treatments.name: Vertical Partial Laryngectomy (Open Cordectomy / Laryngofissure)

evidence_level.rationale: Well-established open surgical approach with decades of outcomes data. 2025 multi-institutional study showed VPL 5-year OS 91.9% for T2N0M0 (n=310), superior to SCPL-CHEP/CHP (83.8%, p=0.022). NCCN-listed treatment option for T2 glottic cancer.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 91.9% for VPL in T2N0M0 (significantly better than SCPL, p=0.022). 5-year CSS 92.8%. Excellent oncologic outcomes with the advantage of histological margin assessment. Preserves laryngeal framework and allows voice rehabilitation.

accessibility.rationale: Available at most medical centers with otolaryngology-head and neck surgery departments. Less technically demanding than SCPL. Requires open surgical approach but standard surgical equipment. More widely available than TLM in some regions.

safety_profile.rationale: Requires external neck incision and laryngofissure. Temporary tracheostomy often needed. Voice quality altered but functional. More morbidity than TLM but less than total laryngectomy. Wound complications in 5-10%. Avoids all radiation side effects.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Surgical approach independent of molecular profile. Patient selection based on tumor extent and suitability for conservation surgery.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Open partial laryngectomy through an external approach. The thyroid cartilage is split (laryngofissure) to expose the endolarynx, and the involved vocal cord(s) are excised with adequate margins while preserving the laryngeal framework, at least one arytenoid, and the cricoid cartilage.

key_evidence.study_name: Oncological outcomes of surgical management for T2N0M0 glottic laryngeal SCC — VPL subgroup
key_evidence.journal: World Journal of Otorhinolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery

treatments.availability: Available at most medical centers with head and neck surgery services

treatments.name: Supracricoid Partial Laryngectomy (SCPL-CHEP/CHP)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple large institutional series with long-term follow-up. 2025 World Journal of Otorhinolaryngology study of 533 T2N0M0 patients showed 5-year DFS 85.3% for partial laryngectomy. 5-year local control of 100% for T2 in SCPL series. Established alternative to RT.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 90.1% for partial laryngectomy in T2N0M0 (n=400). 5-year CSS 91.1%. Organ preservation rate 97% with SCPL. Comparable or superior oncologic outcomes to RT with the advantage of definitive pathological assessment and margin evaluation.

accessibility.rationale: Requires specialized head and neck surgical expertise. Available at major academic centers but limited in community settings. Technique demands significant surgical experience with laryngeal reconstruction (cricohyoidopexy or cricohyoidoepiglottopexy).

safety_profile.rationale: More invasive than TLM or RT. Requires temporary tracheostomy and nasogastric feeding. Aspiration risk during swallowing rehabilitation (2-4 weeks). Voice quality altered but functional speech preserved. Permanent tracheostomy rare (<5%). Avoids radiation toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker selection required. Surgical resection is independent of PD-L1 or EGFR status. Patient selection based on tumor extent and patient fitness for open surgery.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Open partial laryngectomy that removes the thyroid cartilage, true and false vocal cords, and paraglottic space while preserving at least one arytenoid cartilage, the cricoid cartilage, and the hyoid bone. Reconstruction via cricohyoidoepiglottopexy (CHEP) or cricohyoidopexy (CHP) restores airway, phonation, and deglutition.

key_evidence.study_name: Oncological outcomes of surgical management for T2N0M0 glottic laryngeal SCC
key_evidence.journal: World Journal of Otorhinolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery

treatments.availability: Available at academic medical centers with experienced head and neck surgeons

treatments.name: Total Laryngectomy (Salvage Setting)

evidence_level.rationale: Well-established salvage procedure for recurrent or persistent glottic cancer after primary RT or CRT failure. 5-year OS 54-86% depending on recurrence extent. NCCN-recommended salvage approach. Extensive surgical experience worldwide. For T2N0M0 primary: 5-year DFS 80.7%.

survival_benefit.rationale: Effective salvage with 5-year OS 54-86%. For primary treatment of T2N0M0: 5-year OS 86.0% (comparable to partial laryngectomy). However, results in permanent voice loss and stoma. Reserved for salvage or when organ preservation fails. Not first-line for T2N0.

accessibility.rationale: Universally available at all hospitals with head and neck surgery capability. Standard surgical procedure with well-established technique. Voice rehabilitation options (TEP, electrolarynx, esophageal speech) widely available.

safety_profile.rationale: Major surgery with permanent loss of natural voice and permanent tracheostomy. Pharyngocutaneous fistula in 15-25%. Stomal complications. Hypothyroidism. Dysphagia. Significant impact on quality of life. Psychological burden of permanent airway alteration.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Definitive surgical approach independent of molecular profile. Reserved for treatment failure or when organ preservation is not feasible.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical removal of the larynx including the hyoid bone, pre-epiglottic space, and all intrinsic laryngeal structures. The trachea is brought to the skin as a permanent stoma for breathing. Pharyngeal closure restores swallowing. Voice rehabilitation via tracheoesophageal puncture (TEP) with voice prosthesis, electrolarynx, or esophageal speech.

key_evidence.study_name: Oncological outcomes of surgical management for T2N0M0 glottic laryngeal SCC — TL subgroup
key_evidence.journal: World Journal of Otorhinolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery

treatments.availability: Universally available — typically reserved for salvage after organ preservation failure

treatments.name: Cetuximab + Radiation Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: Bonner et al. Phase 3 trial demonstrated improved OS with cetuximab+RT in locally advanced HNSCC. Secondary analysis showed 2-year laryngeal preservation 87.9% vs 85.7%. However, RTOG 1016 showed cetuximab inferior to cisplatin (5-year OS 77.9% vs 84.6%). Role now limited to cisplatin-ineligible patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: In the Bonner trial, median OS improved from 29.3 to 49.0 months. 2-year laryngeal preservation 87.9%. However, proven inferior to cisplatin+RT in head-to-head comparison (RTOG 1016). For T2N0 glottic cancer, RT alone may be sufficient, making cetuximab addition of marginal benefit.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for locally advanced HNSCC in combination with radiation. Cetuximab is widely available. Weekly IV infusion during radiation is logistically straightforward. Primarily reserved for patients with contraindications to cisplatin.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than cisplatin-based CRT. Key toxicities include acneiform rash (80%, may correlate with response), infusion reactions (3% severe), hypomagnesemia, and radiation dermatitis enhancement. No nephrotoxicity or ototoxicity. Rash manageable with prophylactic antibiotics.

biomarker_match.rationale: This patient has EGFR overexpression, which is the target of cetuximab. However, EGFR overexpression alone is not a validated predictive biomarker for cetuximab benefit in HNSCC. Response correlates more with acneiform rash severity than EGFR expression level.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cetuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody that binds the extracellular domain of EGFR, blocking ligand binding and receptor activation. This inhibits downstream RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling, reducing proliferation and enhancing radiosensitivity. Also induces antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC).

key_evidence.study_name: Bonner Trial — Cetuximab + RT in Locally Advanced HNSCC

biomarker_requirements: EGFR expression (target present in >90% of HNSCC, not used for selection)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — available for cisplatin-ineligible patients with locally advanced HNSCC

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Platinum + 5-FU (KEYNOTE-048 Regimen, for R/M Setting)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-048 Phase 3 trial (n=882) with 5-year follow-up established pembrolizumab + chemo as first-line standard for R/M HNSCC. In CPS>=20: 5-year OS 19.9% vs 7.4% with EXTREME. FDA approved for first-line R/M HNSCC. Landmark practice-changing trial.

survival_benefit.rationale: Transformative in R/M HNSCC but not applicable to primary treatment of early-stage disease. This patient has curable T2N0 glottic cancer. Relevant only as salvage option if disease recurs and becomes metastatic. 5-year OS 19.9% in R/M setting reflects the poor prognosis of that population, not this patient.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved first-line for R/M HNSCC. Pembrolizumab, cisplatin/carboplatin, and 5-FU are all widely available. Well-established clinical infrastructure for administration.

safety_profile.rationale: Combines chemotherapy toxicity (myelosuppression, mucositis, nausea) with immune-related AEs. Better overall safety profile than EXTREME (cetuximab + chemo). Grade 3+ AEs in ~70% but manageable. Not justified for current early-stage presentation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has PD-L1 CPS 1-5, within the CPS>=1 population that showed benefit. Greatest benefit in CPS>=20 (which this patient does not meet). Moderate predicted benefit based on CPS level.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Triple combination: pembrolizumab blocks PD-1 checkpoint to reactivate anti-tumor immunity; cisplatin/carboplatin induces DNA crosslinks causing tumor cell death and immunogenic cell death releasing neoantigens; 5-fluorouracil inhibits thymidylate synthase disrupting DNA/RNA synthesis. Chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death may synergize with checkpoint inhibition.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-048 — Pembrolizumab-based First-Line for R/M HNSCC

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for first-line R/M HNSCC; not indicated for primary early-stage disease

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab (Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant, KEYNOTE-689 Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-689 Phase 3 trial (n=714) demonstrated significant EFS benefit with perioperative pembrolizumab for resectable LA HNSCC including laryngeal cancer. 3-year EFS 58.2% vs 44.9% in CPS>=1 (HR 0.70, p=0.003). FDA approved June 2025 for resectable LA HNSCC. However, trial enrolled stage III-IVA, not stage II.

survival_benefit.rationale: 3-year EFS improvement of 13.3% absolute in CPS>=1 patients. Significant major pathological responses. However, this patient has stage II disease with already excellent prognosis (5-year OS >85%), so the absolute benefit of adding immunotherapy would be marginal. Trial population was stage III-IVA, limiting extrapolation.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved since June 2025 for resectable LA HNSCC with PD-L1 CPS>=1. Pembrolizumab widely available. However, the approved indication is for locally advanced (stage III-IVA) disease requiring surgery, not for stage II glottic cancer treated with definitive RT. Off-label use would be required.

safety_profile.rationale: Immune-related adverse events including thyroiditis (10-15%), pneumonitis (<5%), hepatitis (<5%), colitis (<5%). Did not affect surgical completion in KEYNOTE-689. Adding immunotherapy toxicity to an already curable stage II patient may not be justified by the marginal benefit.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has PD-L1 CPS 1-5, meeting the CPS>=1 threshold for the FDA-approved indication. Benefit is greater in CPS>=10 (HR 0.66) than CPS>=1 (HR 0.70). This patient's low CPS (1-5) suggests moderate but not maximal predicted benefit from pembrolizumab.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody that blocks PD-1 on T cells, preventing interaction with PD-L1/PD-L2 on tumor cells. This releases the immune checkpoint brake, reactivating T-cell mediated anti-tumor immunity. Neoadjuvant administration primes anti-tumor immune response before definitive treatment.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-689 — Perioperative Pembrolizumab in Resectable LA HNSCC

biomarker_requirements: Resectable locally advanced HNSCC (approved indication is stage III-IVA)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for resectable LA HNSCC (stage III-IVA) with PD-L1 CPS>=1; off-label for stage II

treatments.name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT / SABR, 42.5 Gy in 5 fractions)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 GLoTtic-SABR trial (Sher et al. 2025) demonstrated 92% local control at 3.7 years. Sanguineti et al. reported 100% local control at 4 years with 3-fraction SBRT. Limited to small prospective studies and Phase 2 trials. No Phase 3 comparison to conventional RT yet.

survival_benefit.rationale: 92% local control at 3.7 years in GLoTtic-SABR. 100% local control at 4 years in selected patients (Sanguineti). Voice Handicap Index improved from 57 to 7.5 at one year. Comparable efficacy to conventional RT with dramatically fewer treatment sessions (3-5 vs 30-35 fractions).

accessibility.rationale: Requires specialized SBRT-capable linear accelerator or CyberKnife system with laryngeal immobilization. Available at academic centers and select community practices. Not yet standard of care; typically offered through clinical trials or specialized programs. Limited insurance coverage.

safety_profile.rationale: Variable toxicity across studies. Sher 5-fraction: no grade 3+ toxicity. Sanguineti 3-fraction: 18.2% grade 4 necrosis (associated with smoking and COVID). Cartilage necrosis risk correlates with PTV volume >10 cm3 and continued smoking. Active smokers (25 pack-years in this patient) are higher risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker selection required. Radiation efficacy is independent of PD-L1 or EGFR status. Patient selection based on tumor volume and smoking status rather than molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Ultra-hypofractionated radiation delivering ablative doses in 3-5 fractions using stereotactic precision. High single-fraction doses induce vascular damage and enhanced immunogenic cell death in addition to direct DNA damage, potentially improving tumor control through distinct radiobiological mechanisms compared to conventional fractionation.

key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology, Biology, Physics

notable_side_effects: Mucosal necrosis (up to 18% in some series)
treatments.availability: Investigational — available at select academic centers, primarily through clinical trials

treatments.name: Nivolumab (Salvage/R-M Setting)

evidence_level.rationale: CheckMate 141 Phase 3 trial established nivolumab as standard second-line for R/M HNSCC after platinum failure. Median OS 7.5 vs 5.1 months. 2-year OS 16.9% vs 6.0%. FDA approved since 2016. However, not indicated for primary early-stage disease.

survival_benefit.rationale: Meaningful benefit in R/M setting (HR 0.70) but not applicable to primary treatment of stage II glottic cancer. This patient has curable early-stage disease; nivolumab is a salvage option only. Relevant if disease recurs after primary treatment and becomes platinum-refractory.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for R/M HNSCC progressing on or after platinum-based chemotherapy. Widely available. IV infusion every 2 or 4 weeks. Well-established infrastructure for administration and immune-related AE monitoring.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than cytotoxic chemotherapy. Immune-related AEs in 10-15% (thyroiditis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis). Grade 3-4 treatment-related AEs in ~13%. Response appears independent of PD-L1 status, unlike pembrolizumab.

biomarker_match.rationale: Nivolumab benefit in CheckMate 141 was observed regardless of PD-L1 expression, making the patient's CPS 1-5 less relevant for treatment selection. However, this drug is not applicable to the current early-stage treatment scenario.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab is a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1. By blocking PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, it restores T-cell anti-tumor activity. In HNSCC, this reactivates tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes that have been suppressed by PD-L1-expressing tumor cells in the tumor microenvironment.
biomarker_requirements: FDA approved regardless of PD-L1 status for R/M HNSCC after platinum failure
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for R/M HNSCC after platinum failure; not indicated for primary early-stage disease

treatments.name: Intensity-Modulated Proton Therapy (IMPT)

evidence_level.rationale: Small prospective series (n=15, T1-2N0 glottic SCC) demonstrated 100% OS and metastasis-free survival at 2 years with exceptional tolerability. Pencil beam scanning proton therapy for single vocal cord irradiation shows promise. No Phase 3 data comparing to photon-based RT. At least 16 ongoing trials.

survival_benefit.rationale: 100% overall survival and metastasis-free survival at 1 and 2 years. Laryngeal function preservation rates excellent. Theoretical dosimetric advantage over photon IMRT with reduced dose to surrounding normal tissues. Single vocal cord irradiation technique may optimize voice preservation.

accessibility.rationale: Extremely limited availability — requires proton therapy facility (fewer than 45 centers in the US). Very expensive. Long wait times at most centers. Insurance coverage inconsistent for laryngeal cancer. Geographic barrier for most patients.

safety_profile.rationale: Exceptional tolerability — no grade 3+ acute or late toxicity reported. Mean grade I events 4.1 per patient, grade II events 1.0 per patient. Reduced dose to surrounding critical structures (carotid arteries, esophagus, pharyngeal constrictors) compared to photon RT. Voice quality significantly improves over time.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker selection required. Proton therapy advantage is dosimetric, not molecular. Benefit is tissue-sparing, independent of PD-L1 or EGFR status.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beams deposit energy at the Bragg peak, allowing precise dose delivery to the tumor with rapid dose fall-off beyond the target. Intensity modulation enables conformal dose distributions that spare surrounding normal tissues (carotid arteries, pharyngeal constrictors, esophagus) while delivering tumoricidal doses to the vocal cord lesion.

key_evidence.study_name: IMPT for Early-Stage Glottic Cancer — Laryngeal Function Preservation

treatments.availability: Limited — available only at proton therapy centers (fewer than 45 in US)

treatments.name: Xevinapant (Debio 1143) + Cisplatin + RT

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data showed 54% locoregional control at 18 months vs 33% with CRT alone — a dramatic improvement. Phase 3 TrilynX trial (NCT04459715) is ongoing for locally advanced HNSCC. No data specific to early-stage glottic cancer. Novel mechanism of IAP inhibition with strong preclinical rationale.

survival_benefit.rationale: Phase 2 locoregional control 54% vs 33% is promising but in locally advanced HNSCC, not early glottic cancer. If Phase 3 confirms benefit, could represent paradigm shift in CRT-based regimens. Limited applicability to T2N0 where RT alone achieves >80% control.

accessibility.rationale: Investigational only. Available exclusively through clinical trials (TrilynX Phase 3). Not FDA approved. Not commercially available. Limited trial sites. Regulatory review pending Phase 3 results.

safety_profile.rationale: Added to cisplatin-based CRT, so inherits all cisplatin toxicities. Xevinapant-specific AEs under characterization in Phase 3. Phase 2 showed manageable toxicity but small sample size. Would represent overtreatment for stage II glottic cancer.

biomarker_match.rationale: No established biomarker for xevinapant response. IAP (inhibitor of apoptosis protein) targeting is independent of PD-L1 and EGFR status. Mechanism may be synergistic with EGFR inhibition but not yet validated.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Xevinapant (Debio 1143) is a small-molecule antagonist of inhibitor of apoptosis proteins (IAPs), specifically cIAP1 and XIAP. By degrading cIAP1 and blocking XIAP, it restores apoptotic signaling, sensitizes tumor cells to cisplatin and radiation-induced death, and activates TNF-alpha-mediated anti-tumor immunity.

key_evidence.study_name: Debio 1143 Phase 2 — IAP Antagonist + CRT in LA HNSCC

treatments.name: Tislelizumab + Afatinib (Neoadjuvant, neoCHANCE-1 Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: neoCHANCE-1 Phase 2 trial (NCT05517330) showed 35% major pathological response (8/23 evaluable patients) with dual PD-1 + EGFR blockade in neoadjuvant setting for locally advanced HNSCC. Published in Nature Communications 2025. Small sample size, single-arm, and enrolled advanced-stage patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: 35% major pathological response is encouraging for a neoadjuvant approach. However, pathological response is a surrogate endpoint; long-term survival data not yet mature. Trial enrolled locally advanced patients, not early-stage T2N0. Dual EGFR + PD-1 blockade is scientifically rational for this EGFR-overexpressing, PD-L1+ patient.

accessibility.rationale: Investigational only. Tislelizumab has limited availability outside of China-based trials. Afatinib is FDA-approved for NSCLC but not for HNSCC. Combination is available only in clinical trial settings. Very limited access globally.

safety_profile.rationale: Combines immune checkpoint inhibitor toxicity (immune-mediated AEs) with EGFR-TKI toxicity (diarrhea, rash, stomatitis). Manageable in trial setting but cumulative toxicity of dual-targeted approach requires monitoring. Not justified for stage II disease with excellent prognosis.

biomarker_match.rationale: Dual targeting of PD-1 and EGFR is highly relevant for this patient with both PD-L1 CPS 1-5 and EGFR overexpression. Afatinib targets EGFR (overexpressed in this patient), and tislelizumab targets PD-1 pathway (patient is PD-L1 positive). Best biomarker match among experimental options.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tislelizumab is an anti-PD-1 antibody engineered to minimize Fc-gamma receptor binding on macrophages, reducing antibody-dependent phagocytosis of T cells. Afatinib is an irreversible pan-ErbB tyrosine kinase inhibitor blocking EGFR, HER2, and HER4. Combined dual pathway blockade aims to overcome resistance mechanisms in EGFR-overexpressing, PD-L1-positive tumors.

key_evidence.study_name: neoCHANCE-1 — Neoadjuvant Tislelizumab + Afatinib in LA HNSCC

clinical_trials.title: VOCAL — Vocal-Cord Only vs Complete Laryngeal Radiation in T1N0 Glottic SCC
biomarker_requirements: T1N0 glottic SCC (not T2, but relevant radiation technique research)
clinical_trials.early_results: Randomized 1:3 to complete larynx RT (n=39) vs vocal-cord only RT (n=116). Assessing non-inferiority of reduced-field RT at 2 years. Results pending. If positive, may extend to T2 glottic cancers and reduce radiation-related toxicity.

clinical_trials.title: TrilynX — Xevinapant (Debio 1143) + Cisplatin + RT vs Placebo + Cisplatin + RT in LA HNSCC
biomarker_requirements: Not specific to early glottic cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase 2 showed 54% locoregional control at 18 months vs 33% with CRT alone. If Phase 3 confirms, xevinapant could become standard addition to CRT for locally advanced HNSCC. Novel IAP-antagonist mechanism. Primary endpoint: event-free survival.

clinical_trials.title: neoCHANCE-1 — Neoadjuvant Tislelizumab + Afatinib in LA HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: 35% major pathological response (8/23 evaluable patients) with dual PD-1 + EGFR blockade. Published Nature Communications 2025. Demonstrates feasibility of combined immune checkpoint + EGFR inhibition in neoadjuvant setting. Relevant to this patient's EGFR-overexpressing, PD-L1+ profile.

clinical_trials.title: Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy for T1-T2 Glottic Larynx Cancer (GLoTtic-SABR)
clinical_trials.early_results: 92% local control at 3.7 years with 42.5 Gy in 5 fractions. No grade 3+ toxicity in 5-fraction arm. Voice Handicap Index improved from 57 to 7.5 at 1 year. Supports SBRT as viable alternative to conventional RT with dramatically fewer treatment sessions.

clinical_trials.title: Perioperative Pembrolizumab in Resectable Locally Advanced HNSCC
biomarker_requirements: Resectable stage III-IVA HNSCC (laryngeal, hypopharyngeal, oral cavity)
clinical_trials.early_results: 3-year EFS 58.2% vs 44.9% (HR 0.70, p=0.003) in CPS>=1. In CPS>=10: 3-year EFS 59.8% vs 45.9% (HR 0.66, p=0.004). FDA approved June 2025 for resectable LA HNSCC with PD-L1 CPS>=1. Did not compromise surgical completion. Paradigm shift for perioperative immunotherapy in HNSCC.

clinical_trials.title: Adjuvant Nivolumab After Surgery and RT for Resected High-Risk HNSCC
biomarker_requirements: High risk of relapse (positive margins or extranodal extension)
clinical_trials.early_results: Met primary endpoint of disease-free survival in 2025. Adjuvant nivolumab after surgery and post-operative RT improves DFS in high-risk resected HNSCC. Could expand to intermediate-risk patients in future studies.

combination_strategies.rationale: RTOG 91-11 established concurrent CRT as superior to RT alone or induction chemo+RT for laryngeal preservation (88% vs 75% vs 70% at 2 years). 5-year LRC 88.5% vs 72.8% with RT alone for T2 glottic cancer. However, significantly more toxic than RT alone. For T2N0 with favorable prognosis, the added toxicity may outweigh marginal benefit over RT alone. Best reserved for unfavorable T2 features (anterior commissure involvement, impaired mobility approaching T3).

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Cetuximab (400 mg/m2 loading then 250 mg/m2 weekly)
combination_strategies.rationale: Cetuximab+RT improved OS vs RT alone (49 vs 29.3 months, HR 0.74). Relevant for this EGFR-overexpressing patient. However, RTOG 1016 definitively showed cetuximab+RT inferior to cisplatin+RT (5-year OS 77.9% vs 84.6%). Current role limited to cisplatin-ineligible patients. For this 45-year-old ECOG 0 patient who can tolerate cisplatin, cetuximab+RT is a second-choice option. May be considered if patient strongly prefers avoiding cisplatin toxicity.

KEYNOTE-689 demonstrated significant EFS benefit with perioperative pembrolizumab in resectable LA HNSCC (HR 0.70 for CPS>=1). This patient's PD-L1 CPS 1-5 meets the approved biomarker threshold. However, trial enrolled stage III-IVA disease; this patient has stage II with excellent prognosis. Adding immunotherapy to a highly curable early-stage cancer requires careful risk-benefit consideration. May be relevant if upstaged at surgery.

Dual PD-1 + EGFR blockade achieved 35% major pathological response in LA HNSCC. Scientifically rational for this patient with both PD-L1 CPS 1-5 and EGFR overexpression — simultaneously targeting both actionable pathways. Afatinib provides irreversible EGFR inhibition (potentially superior to cetuximab). Early-stage investigation; not applicable to primary treatment of T2N0 glottic cancer outside clinical trials.

Adding xevinapant to standard CRT showed 54% vs 33% locoregional control at 18 months — the most impressive improvement over CRT alone in recent HNSCC trials. IAP inhibition restores apoptotic pathways and enhances both chemo- and radiosensitivity. If Phase 3 confirms, this could become the new CRT backbone. Not yet applicable to early-stage disease but watch for expansion studies.

supportive_care.evidence: 25 pack-year smoking history is the most significant modifiable risk factor. Continued smoking during radiation increases grade 3+ mucositis (significantly associated), cartilage necrosis (18.2% in SBRT smokers), osteoradionecrosis, hospitalization, and reduces treatment efficacy. Cessation after diagnosis associated with 61% reduction in mortality risk. Varenicline or combination NRT recommended.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced treatment toxicity, improved local control, decreased risk of second primary tumors, improved overall survival, better voice outcomes post-treatment, reduced risk of SBRT/RT complications

supportive_care.approach: Speech-Language Pathology (Pre- and Post-Treatment Voice Rehabilitation)
supportive_care.evidence: Pre-treatment baseline voice assessment is critical. Post-treatment voice therapy includes 10 speech pathology sessions of direct and indirect voice intervention. 40-45% of TLM patients achieve normal or near-normal voice. Voice Handicap Index monitoring tracks recovery. Multidisciplinary involvement improves functional outcomes. UK national guidelines recommend SLP assessment for all head and neck cancer patients.
supportive_care.benefit: Optimized voice quality preservation, early detection of voice deterioration, improved quality of life, functional communication restoration, psychological wellbeing support

supportive_care.evidence: Prophylactic swallowing exercises during radiation maintain pharyngeal muscle function and reduce post-treatment dysphagia severity. Video-fluorographic swallowing assessment recommended post-treatment. Oral nutritional supplements maintain caloric/protein intake during treatment. Maintaining oral intake during RT has positive impact on swallowing outcomes. Feeding tube placement reserved for high-risk patients or nutritional deficiency.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevention of late dysphagia, maintained oral intake, reduced weight loss during treatment, prevention of aspiration, improved quality of life

supportive_care.approach: Hypertension Management Optimization During Cancer Treatment
supportive_care.evidence: Patient has pre-existing hypertension. Cisplatin causes nephrotoxicity and can worsen hypertension. Cetuximab and immunotherapy agents can cause hypertension. Anti-hypertensive medication adjustment may be needed during treatment. ACE inhibitors may have radioprotective properties. Blood pressure monitoring intensified during chemoradiation.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced cardiovascular risk during treatment, prevention of renal complications with cisplatin, optimized drug interactions, maintained treatment adherence

supportive_care.approach: Mucositis Prevention and Management (Oral Care Protocol)
supportive_care.evidence: Grade 2-3 mucositis occurs in 30-60% of patients receiving radiation to the larynx. Prophylactic oral care including saline rinses, soft toothbrush, and avoiding irritants reduces severity. Pain management with topical lidocaine and systemic analgesics. Continued smoking significantly worsens mucositis severity and duration.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced treatment breaks, maintained nutrition, pain control, prevention of secondary infections, treatment completion

supportive_care.evidence: Voice rehabilitation programs improve psychological wellbeing. Laryngeal cancer patients experience anxiety about voice changes, treatment decisions (organ preservation vs surgery), and cancer recurrence. Pre-treatment counseling about expected voice changes improves coping. Support groups for laryngeal cancer patients available through organizations like the Voice Foundation.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced anxiety and depression, improved treatment adherence, better quality of life, informed decision-making about organ preservation, coping strategies for voice changes
